lesson.menu_desc: Pushback & Diplomatic Disagreement — Modal Perfects — 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 3 — Navigating Tough Conversations — Pushback, Objections, and Diplomatic Disagreement
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 3 — Navigating Tough Conversations

listenings.text: Hi Victor, this is Priya Sharma calling from the Mumbai office. I have been reviewing the Q1 growth proposal you sent over last week and I have some concerns I wanted to share before Thursday's call. First, the headcount increase you are requesting for LATAM, I understand the rationale, but given that we had friction with HR over the last hiring round, the board might have concerns about timing. They should have been consulted earlier in the process, frankly. Second, the conversion rate targets you have set are ambitious. I am not saying they are unrealistic, but the EMEA team tried something similar last year and they could not sustain it beyond the first quarter. One could argue that a more conservative target would build more credibility with leadership. Third, and this is the one I want to discuss most, the budget reallocation from digital to field operations. I take your point about needing local presence, but to some extent, we might be undermining the digital traction we built in Q3. I think we should have kept at least sixty percent of the digital budget intact. Look, I am not trying to block your proposal. I think there is a strong case here. But I want to make sure we put forward a version that leadership cannot push back on. Let us discuss Thursday. Thanks.

extra_audio.text: First, listen carefully and acknowledge the other person's concern. Second, paraphrase their objection to show you understand. Third, present your perspective using diplomatic language. Fourth, propose a compromise or alternative solution. Fifth, confirm alignment and agree on next steps.
